title: AI and Generative AI as ADM Tools
description: Use of AI (including Generative AI) for training and output is expressly identified as an 'automated decision-making' tool. Where its use has legal effects on the data subject or significantly impacts the data subject, the ADM Restrictions apply. Source: JPDP ADMP Guideline (PCP No. 3/2025 substantive content).
rule: Use of AI and Generative AI by data controllers, where it has legal effects on the data subject or significantly impacts the data subject, is subject to the ADM Restrictions (Right to Refuse, Right to Information, Right to Human Review).
scopingNotes: Not all use of AI involves automated decision-making and profiling
AI chatbot generating responses based on patterns learned from training data, without making decisions about users, is generally outside the trigger
AI used to analyse complex datasets to identify patterns, predict outcomes, and make decisions about individuals is within the trigger

dataProtectionConcerns.description: AI development uses large amounts of data including personal and sensitive information. Users may not know how their data is processed and may struggle to exercise rights. The Right to Information addresses this.

Training data, the trained model, and output may all contain personal information. Difficult to access or correct; risk of disclosure or output influencing outcomes for individuals in response to particular prompts. The ADM Restrictions help address this.

additionalAiSafeguards.name: Dignity and accuracy
additionalAiSafeguards.rule: When using AI for profiling that significantly affects personal rights and benefits, AI must be used in a way that respects the dignity of individuals, maintains the utmost accuracy of output, recognises the limitations of AI predictions/recommendations/judgments, and considers possible disadvantages — and must not be used for inappropriate purposes.

additionalAiSafeguards.name: No manipulation
additionalAiSafeguards.rule: Do not develop, provide, or use AI systems and services to manipulate human decision-making, recognition, or emotion, or to control individuals without their awareness. Take countermeasures against the risk of over-dependence on AI (automated bias).

additionalAiSafeguards.name: Risk assessment before deployment
additionalAiSafeguards.rule: Before deploying AI solutions, decide commercial objectives and weigh them against the risks of using AI in the organisation's decision-making.

additionalAiSafeguards.name: Operator training
additionalAiSafeguards.rule: Where the AI system is intended for autonomous decision-making, provide training or clear explanation so organisations using the AI system properly understand how it operates.

additionalAiSafeguards.name: Not the only factor
additionalAiSafeguards.rule: AI must not be implemented as the only factor in policies and/or decisions concerning people. Prevent racism and avoid actions harming people.

additionalAiSafeguards.name: Meaningful human review
additionalAiSafeguards.rule: Human reviewers must check the system's recommendation and not just apply to individuals automatically. Reviewer involvement must be active — not for show. Reviewers must have actual meaningful influence (authority and competence) to go against the automated recommendation. They must evaluate and interpret the recommendation, consider all input data, and take into account other additional factors.

exampleHighImpactUseCases: Financial Services — generative AI determining creditworthiness, affecting access to loans/mortgages/credit cards
Healthcare Diagnostics — AI predicting disease likelihood, impacting insurance premiums and eligibility
Law Enforcement and Surveillance — AI identifying individuals in public spaces, impacting privacy and civil liberties
Social Benefits and Welfare — AI determining eligibility for unemployment insurance, disability benefits, welfare programs; errors or bias can wrongfully deny benefits
crossRepoLink: AI-Governance repo controls — ADMP triggers obligations under NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act parallels, ISO 42001
